coredata.dc:description: The development of innovative techniques for latent fingerprint (LFP) analysis and anti-counterfeiting applications is crucial for addressing various societal challenges. Quantum dot (QD)-based fluorescent staining offers a promising approach due to its high sensitivity. However, the toxicity of traditional heavy metal-containing QDs necessitates the exploration of eco-friendly alternatives. This study presents a facile and environmentally sustainable method for synthesizing heteroatom (nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium)-doped carbon quantum dots (N-P-K@CQDs) derived from the flower extract of Pyrostegia venusta. Characterization revealed spherical CQDs with an average size of approximately 4.7 nm, a quantum yield of 25 %, and excellent water solubility, photostability, and greenish-fluorescent properties. N-P-K@CQDs were incorporated into a cornstarch nanocomposite phosphor, which exhibited greenish fluorescence under UV excitation. The nanocomposite was utilized as a fluorescent marker for LFP analysis using a customized smartphone-based dactyloscope. Python-based image processing algorithms were employed to enhance and analyze the fluorescence images of the developed LFPs. Additionally; a proof-of-concept anti-counterfeiting tag was demonstrated using CQDs-based ink, enabling the detection of concealed security marks. The findings highlight the potential of eco-friendly N-P-K@CQDs as a versatile platform for both LFP analysis and anti-counterfeiting applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graphical representation of synthesis of Pyrostegia venusta derived hetero-atom doped fluorescent CQDs (N-P-K@CQDs).
UV–vis comparison spectra of microwave power dependent synthesis of N-P-K@CQDs derived from Pyrostegia venusta.
(a) A comparison of fluorescence spectra on the microwave power dependent synthesis of CQDs. Digital photographs of concerned samples under (b) daylight and (c) UV exposure (365 nm, 60 W).

(a)-(b) Typical high-resolution TEM micrographs of N-P-K@CQDs derived from Pyrostegia venusta. (c) Displaying the d-spacing in HR-TEM images. (d) The histogram of particle size distribution along with Gaussian fit. (e) SAED patterns and (f) energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDX) profile of as-synthesized N-P-K@CQDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of Pyrostegia venusta derived N-P-K@CQDs. High-resolution (b) C 1 s spectra, (c) O 1 s spectra, (d) N1s spectra, (e) P 2p spectra and (f) K 2p spectra.
(a) Excitation-wavelength dependent FL spectra of N,P,K-co-doped CQDs. (b) corresponding contour plot between excitation v/s emission wavelength.
CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage) chromaticity coordinates according to CIE 1931 and 1976 from FL spectra of Pyrostegia venusta derived N,P,K-co-doped CQDs.

(a) Schematic core–shell structure of synthesized N-P-K@CQDs. (b) Schematic energy band diagram representing proposed fluorescence mechanism of CQDs under the influence of protic solvent (dispersed in DI water) and UV exposure.

Digital photographs of latent fingerprints collected from various surfaces such as laptop, CD, fridge handle, mobile etc. using custom made dactyloscope during daylight and under UV light (365 nm, 18 W, captured using a smart phone camera).

Digital image processing of a thumb impression using N-P-K@CQDs/cornstarch nanocomposite phosphors from a latent fingerprint on a rough and dirty glass surface. (a) input original image, (b) Gaussian blurred, (c) sharpness adjustment, (d) grayscale, (e) CLAHE, (f) binarization, (g) second level details as per Galton-Henry classification obtain from binary image (dot, short ridge, crossover, delta, eye, bifurcation, spur, island).

The security applications of N-P-K@CQDs-based anti-counterfeiting ink. (a)-(f) Daylight and fluorescent images of painting and popular cartoon character Doraemon with clearly visible security mark or paint.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of innovative techniques for latent fingerprint (LFP) analysis and anti-counterfeiting applications is crucial for addressing various societal challenges. Quantum dot (QD)-based fluorescent staining offers a promising approach due to its high sensitivity. However, the toxicity of traditional heavy metal-containing QDs necessitates the exploration of eco-friendly alternatives. This study presents a facile and environmentally sustainable method for synthesizing heteroatom (nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium)-doped carbon quantum dots (N-P-K@CQDs) derived from the flower extract of Pyrostegia venusta. Characterization revealed spherical CQDs with an average size of approximately 4.7 nm, a quantum yield of 25 %, and excellent water solubility, photostability, and greenish-fluorescent properties. N-P-K@CQDs were incorporated into a cornstarch nanocomposite phosphor, which exhibited greenish fluorescence under UV excitation. The nanocomposite was utilized as a fluorescent marker for LFP analysis using a customized smartphone-based dactyloscope. Python-based image processing algorithms were employed to enhance and analyze the fluorescence images of the developed LFPs. Additionally; a proof-of-concept anti-counterfeiting tag was demonstrated using CQDs-based ink, enabling the detection of concealed security marks. The findings highlight the potential of eco-friendly N-P-K@CQDs as a versatile platform for both LFP analysis and anti-counterfeiting applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fingerprint impressions are a vital tool in personal identification, extensively used in forensics, law enforcement, access control, and medical diagnostics [1–4]. Fingerprints are especially significant in criminal investigations, where they help identify perpetrators, locate missing individuals, and establish personal identities. These impressions, known as latent fingerprints (LFPs), are formed when a finger makes contact with a surface, leaving behind residues of eccrine sweat, sebaceous oils, and other secretions [5–7]. However, LFPs are often invisible to the naked eye, making their detection challenging. Conventional techniques, such as cyanoacrylate fuming, iodine fuming, ninhydrin spraying, powder dusting, and vacuum metal deposition, are commonly employed to visualize LFPs on various forensic surfaces. While effective to some extent, these methods suffer from several limitations, including low sensitivity, poor contrast, background interference, and potential toxicity [8–11]. Nevertheless, these techniques have a number of drawbacks, such as limited specificity, low sensitivity, poor contrast, and background interference. On the other hand, counterfeiting is a serious problem since it affects so many people, businesses, and governments [12]. In this regard, the need for anti-counterfeiting measures that make authentic objects harder to replicate and simpler to recognize has grown on significant and valuable products such as banknotes, luxury items, tickets, drugs, public certifications, documents etc. [13]. The increasing prevalence of counterfeiting has prompted the need for more advanced and secure anti-counterfeiting measures. These measures aim to make genuine products more difficult to replicate while allowing for quick and reliable verification of authenticity. As a result, there is a growing demand for innovative, high-sensitivity, and eco-friendly technologies that can simultaneously address the challenges of forensic fingerprint detection and anti-counterfeiting measures.

In recent years, nanoparticles have emerged as a valuable tool in the development of novel techniques for LFP detection and anti-counterfeiting. Nanoparticles possess unique optical, electrical, and chemical properties that can be harnessed to enhance the visibility of LFPs on a wide range of surfaces. Among these, quantum dots (QDs) have gained prominence due to their strong fluorescence and tunable properties. However, most commonly used QDs, such as cadmium-based QDs, contain toxic heavy metals that pose environmental and health risks. This has led researchers to seek alternative, non-toxic materials that can offer high accuracy and sensitivity without the negative environmental impacts.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a novel member of the carbon-based material family, have recently gained attention due to their favorable properties, including high fluorescence, biocompatibility, environmental friendliness, and cost-effectiveness [14–20]. CQDs are produced from carbon-rich materials, making them a greener alternative to traditional QDs. These unique characteristics have made CQDs a promising candidate for various applications, including biosensing, bioimaging, optoelectronic devices, and energy-related technologies [21–26]. Despite being extensively studied in biosensing and bioimaging, CQDs are relatively new in forensic science and technology and first reported in 2015 by Kelarakis et al. [27]. Since this pioneering study, there has been limited research on the use of CQDs for detecting LFPs. Therefore, producing non-toxic, affordable, and highly sensitive nanocomposite fluorophores using a novel rapid protocol is crucial.

In this scenario, a simple, inexpensive, large-scale eco-friendly solution built on low-cost precursors must be designed and developed. Since natural biomaterial resources are renewable and biocompatible and can transform biomass waste into valuable materials, they are usually chosen over other organic, inorganic, or synthetic resources in the search for an effective, affordable, and environmentally friendly method of synthesising CQDs [28–33].

Additionally, a wide range of complex plant metabolites, including glycosides, alkaloids, minerals, vitamins, phenolics, terpenoids, flavonoids etc. are present in biomaterials extract and may be helpful in doping different elements during the manufacture of CQD without the need for additional doping components [34,35].

The flower extract of Pyrostegia venusta contains a diverse array of bioactive compounds, including flavonoids (such as quercetin, kaempferol, rutin etc.), phenolic acids (gallic, chlorogenic, caffeic acid etc.), tannins, saponins, terpenoids (oleanolic, betulinic acid etc.), coumarins, and trace amounts of alkaloids and essential oils. These compounds contribute to its potent medicinal properties, including antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and potential anticancer activities. Traditionally, Pyrostegia venusta extracts have been used to treat respiratory issues, skin conditions, and inflammation, with flavonoids, phenolic acids, and tannins playing key roles in its therapeutic effects.

This study addresses several critical research gaps in the field of forensic fingerprint analysis and anti-counterfeiting technologies. First, the need for environmentally friendly and non-toxic quantum dot synthesis is addressed by developing hetero-atom doped CQDs from Pyrostegia venusta flower extract using a facile, microwave-assisted method. This green synthesis avoids the use of heavy metals and hazardous chemicals, making it a sustainable alternative for both forensic and anti-counterfeiting applications. Second, while CQDs have been explored in other scientific fields, their use in LFP detection remains underutilized. This research demonstrates the application of CQDs in a nanocomposite fingerprint-developing powder, enhancing the detection of LFPs with high sensitivity and contrast.

Furthermore, the study explores the use of CQDs in anti-counterfeiting technologies, developing a proof-of-concept for an anti-counterfeiting tag using hydrophilic CQDs as fluorescent ink. This expands the utility of CQDs into the security domain, addressing the growing demand for advanced and secure verification methods for high-value goods. Lastly, the development of a smartphone-based dactyloscope prototype represents a significant innovation in real-time forensic analysis. This portable device utilizes CQD fluorescence under UV light to detect LFPs, providing a practical, user-friendly solution for point-of-care forensic investigations. Coupled with Python-based image processing, the prototype enables remote analysis and data sharing, streamlining the forensic process and enhancing collaboration among experts.

In this study, we report the green synthesis of hetero-atom doped CQDs using the flower extract of Pyrostegia venusta, which contains a variety of bioactive compounds. These CQDs are further applied in a nanocomposite for fingerprint development and as a fluorescent marker in anti-counterfeiting tags. Additionally, we developed a smartphone-based dactyloscope prototype for real-time forensic analysis, coupled with Python-based image processing, enabling enhanced collaboration among forensic experts. By addressing critical gaps related to environmental safety, cost-effectiveness, and forensic efficiency, this study contributes to the advancement of both forensic and anti-counterfeiting technologies.

For green synthesis, healthy orange trumpet vine flowers (scientific name: Pyrostegia venusta, botanical family–Bignoniaceae) as a precursor for CQDs were collected from the nearby locality of the University campus (GPS locations: 30o15′ N, 77o2′ E). Analytical research grade chemicals (acids and solvents) such as hydrochloric acid (HCl), nitric acid (HNO3), acetone, ethanol, methanol, polyethylene glycol (PEG) etc. were all purchased from either Loba Chemie or SRL, India. Other materials obtained through commercial means included Whatman filter paper (grade 1), polytetrafluoroethylene membrane 220 nm syringe filters, corn starch and deionized water obtained using a Milli-Q system (18.2 MΩ.cm, Millipore, France). Fresh aqua regia solution (HCl: HNO3 in 3:1) was used to clean the glassware used during the experiments. No further purification was done; all chemicals were used exactly as they were delivered.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green-synthesis of Pyrostegia venusta derived heteroatom doped carbon quantum dots (N-P-K@CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Heteroatom doped carbon quantum dots (N-P-K@CQDs), a highly fluorescent nanomaterial, were synthesized using a one-step microwave-assisted method. This study presents a comprehensive synthesis protocol starting from the preparation of the Pyrostegia venusta flower extract to the final isolation and storage of the carbon quantum dots. The synthesis process began with the preparation of Pyrostegia venusta flower extract (PVFE). Fresh flowers were initially air-dried for several days until complete dehydration was achieved. The dried flowers were subsequently ground into a fine powder using a domestic grinder or pestle and mortar. For the extraction, 10 g of the powdered Pyrostegia venusta flowers were mixed with 100 mL of double-distilled water in a beaker. The mixture was then heated on a hot plate equipped with a magnetic stirrer at a temperature range of 70–80 °C, with continuous stirring for approximately one hour. Following the heating process, the mixture was allowed to cool to room temperature. The cooled solution was filtered multiple times using Whatman qualitative filter paper (grade 1) to remove particulates, and then centrifuged to further clarify the solution. This process yielded a dark yellowish solution with a pH of 7.2, which was designated as the Pyrostegia venusta flower extract (PVFE). The extract was subsequently refrigerated for use in further synthesis steps. For the microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon quantum dots, the PVFE solution, optionally mixed with polyethylene glycol (PEG), was subjected to sonication prior to microwave irradiation. The solution was heated in a domestic microwave oven at power levels ranging from 127.5 W to 750 W for durations of 2 to 5 min. To prevent overheating and potential structural damage to the carbon quantum dots, the microwave heating was interrupted and adjusted at one-minute intervals. This process resulted in a yellowish-brown viscous fluid, which was then dissolved in distilled water or ethanol. The resultant viscous liquid was subjected to repeated centrifugation for 10 min at speeds of 8000–9000 rpm (relative centrifugal force of 7510–9500g) to remove impurities. Post-centrifugation, the supernatant was collected, and any residual microscopic particles were removed by filtration through a 220 nm polytetrafluoroethylene membrane syringe filter. The final solution was freeze-dried using a lyophilizer at −40 °C and stored at 4 °C for future applications. A graphical representation of the entire synthesis process is provided in Fig. 1 .

To prepare a nanocomposite luminous powder from the optimal N-P-K@CQDs synthesized via microwave-assisted heating at a medium–low power setting of 360 W, the following procedure was employed. Initially, the N-P-K@CQDs, characterized by their optimal synthesis conditions, were selected for integration into the nanocomposite powder. In a mortar, the CQDs were combined with cornstarch, which served as the primary matrix for the nanocomposite. Cornstarch was chosen due to its compatibility with the CQDs and its effectiveness in creating a stable matrix for the luminescent material. Double-distilled water was then added dropwise to the mixture of cornstarch and N-P-K@CQDs. This water acted as a binding agent, facilitating the even distribution of the CQDs throughout the cornstarch. The mixture was subjected to ultrasonic agitation at room temperature for a duration of 10 to 15 min. The ultrasonic treatment was crucial as it promoted thorough mixing and ensured uniform dispersion of the N-P-K@CQDs within the cornstarch matrix, preventing clumping and ensuring consistent luminescence properties throughout the powder. After the ultrasonic agitation, the reaction mixture was allowed to air-dry. The drying process resulted in the formation of a pale-yellow powder. This powder demonstrated pronounced luminescent properties, making it suitable for applications as a fingerprint-developing nanocomposite powder. The luminescence of the powder was evaluated under appropriate lighting conditions to confirm its effectiveness in highlighting latent fingerprints. The resulting nanocomposite powder was thus prepared for potential use in forensic applications, leveraging the enhanced visibility provided by the N-P-K@CQDs' luminescent properties to improve the detection and analysis of latent fingerprints.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of customized smartphone based dactyloscope for applications in point-of-investigation
ce:section.ce:para: A specialized dactyloscope prototype has been developed for smartphone-based detection of latent fingerprints at the point-of-investigation, offering an innovative, portable, and practical solution for forensic applications. The prototype has compact dimensions of 33.5 cm x 19.5 cm x 26.5 cm (W x L x H), making it suitable for use in the field. The entire structure is coated with black chart paper to reduce light interference and enhance the contrast needed for fingerprint visualization, ensuring clearer imaging results. At the heart of this design is an ultraviolet (UV) tube with specifications of 11 W and 365 nm wavelength, strategically placed inside the prototype. This UV light source enables the excitation of fluorescent powders or other luminescent materials used to enhance LFPs, improving visibility on both porous and non-porous surfaces. The choice of 365 nm UV light ensures effective interaction with a wide variety of fingerprint-enhancing substances, while also being safe for both the user and the environment. To integrate modern technology and leverage the high-resolution capabilities of smartphone cameras (iPhone13, 12MP TrueDepth camera), the prototype includes two strategically positioned holes on its top surface. These holes serve as mounts for the smartphone, allowing for precise alignment of the camera with the viewing area. The holes also enable the use of color filters over the smartphone camera, enhancing the contrast and visibility of the developed fingerprints, particularly when working with fluorescent powders or dyes. This setup ensures that LFPs can be effectively visualized and captured, regardless of the surface type. Photographs of the latent fingerprints are captured using a smartphone camera, specifically an iPhone 13, which is known for its high-quality imaging capabilities. Once captured, the images undergo further processing to enhance the details of the fingerprints. This image processing can be conducted using readily available smartphone applications, providing quick and convenient results for investigators in the field. For more advanced and customized analysis, a Python-based image processing algorithm has been developed, which allows for tailored enhancements based on specific forensic needs. This smartphone-based dactyloscope offers a practical and versatile tool for forensic investigators, demonstrating a wide range of applications. Its ability to effectively visualize LFPs on nearly all types of surfaces, combined with the ease of image capture and processing using a smartphone, makes it a valuable addition to point-of-investigation forensic tools. The portability and adaptability of this device ensure that it can be used in various forensic scenarios, from crime scenes to laboratory analysis, providing a robust, efficient, and user-friendly solution for latent fingerprint detection.

The comprehensive characterization of the synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was conducted using an array of sophisticated analytical techniques to thoroughly assess their properties. Ultra-centrifugation was performed with the REMI R8C or CPR-30 Plus centrifuge machine to separate and purify the CQDs from the reaction mixture. The optical properties of the CQDs were examined using a Molecular Devices SpectraMax iD3 spectrophotometer, which recorded UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra across the wavelength ranges of 230–700 nm and 250–700 nm, respectively. To maintain uniform dispersion during the analysis, a REMI MS-500 magnetic stirrer was utilized. The surface functionalization of the CQDs was analysed with a Shimadzu IR Spirit Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer, covering a wavelength range of 4000–400 cm−1, which provided insights into the types of functional groups present on the CQD surfaces. Elemental composition and chemical states were determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with the Physical Electronics PHI 5000 Versa Probe III, spanning an energy range from 0 to 1200 eV. The crystalline structure of the CQDs was assessed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) on a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer with non-monochromatic Cu Kα x-rays, an angular range of 5-90°, and a step size of 0.02°, which allowed for the analysis of their crystallinity and phase composition. Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, conducted on a 500 MHz Bruker Advance Neo NMR spectrometer, provided detailed information on the carbon and hydrogen environments within the CQDs. Surface zeta potential measurements, carried out using an Anton Paar Litesizer 500, assessed the stability and electrostatic interactions of the CQDs in suspension. High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) with a 200 kV accelerating voltage was used to investigate the morphology, size, and shape of the CQDs, while selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns confirmed their crystalline structure. Additionally, Energy-Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDX) performed on a JEOL JEM-2100F electron microscope quantified the relative concentrations of carbon and oxygen, complementing the XPS results and ensuring a thorough understanding of the CQDs' elemental composition. Collectively, these characterization techniques provided a comprehensive analysis of the CQDs, revealing their optical, chemical, structural, and elemental properties essential for evaluating their potential applications in forensic science and anti-counterfeiting technologies.

The relative quantum yield values (ɸ) for quinine sulphate dye, used as a reference standard material, for Pyrostegia venusta flower extract derived CQDs were evaluated. The measurement details can be found from our previous publication [25]. The quantum yield obtained was around 25 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microwave process optimization for green synthesis of N-P-K@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Microwave process parameters have a significant impact on the formation of quantum dots of various sizes and distributions, and hence on their optical properties. Hence, there is a need to look deeper into the effects of process factors such as temperature and time on as-synthesised CQDs. The effects of process variables such as power fluctuation on as-synthesised hetero-atom doped CQDs were then further examined. The threshold absorption peaks gradually shifted to longer wavelengths, including 550.74 nm for low power (127.5 W), 570.30 nm for defrost, 661.94 nm for medium low power (360 W), 688.36 nm for medium power (495 W), 733.85 nm for medium high power (637.5 W), and 742.09 nm for high microwave power (750 W), as illustrated in Fig. 2 (a)-(b).

The red-shift in peak absorption is thought to be produced by the substantially bigger particle size of CQDs created as a result of favourable nucleation conditions at higher processing temperatures. This is owing to the n- π* transitions in the graphitic core and the usual π − π* transition in the conjugated C = C band [36,37]. Meanwhile, the standard Tauc plot (
α h ν = A
h ν -
E g
n
, where α is the absorption coefficient, A is a constant, hν is the energy of an incident photon, Eg is the band gap) was used to estimate the optical band gap. The optical band gap was measured between 2.93 and 3.3 eV, with microwave power variations from 127.5 to 750 W. The details of the calculation can be found from the Fig. S1 in the supplementary document.

Carbon quantum dots, on the other hand, are often identified by their size-dependent fluorescence activity and emission wavelength. Hence, fluorescence emission was investigated in relation to microwave power dependent synthesis of CQDs. Fig. 3 (a) depicts the microwave power dependent fluorescence (FL) spectra of CQDs dispersed in water.

Fig. 3 depicts the microwave process dependent FL spectra of N-P-K@CQDs. The yellow-greenish fluorescence emission upon excitation at 280 nm have been observed. This is also demonstrated by digital images of the various microwave process-dependent water dispersed CQDs samples shown in Fig. 3(b) and (c) under day light and UV (365 nm, 60 W) exposure. Clearly, the FL spectra exhibits microwave power dependency. The deconvoluted spectra further revealed mostly two distinct wide peaks. In general, a wider asymmetric peak spanning from 414-453 nm has been seen following excitation with a wavelength of 250 nm as microwave power varies from 127.5-750 W. The deconvoluted fluorescence spectra, shown in Fig. S2 (a)-(f) and Table S1, revealed primarily two distinct broad peaks for the majority of microwave power, with the exception of low microwave power (127.5 W) and medium high microwave power (637.5 W), where three distinct FL peaks are required to best fit the experimental data. As a consequence, the microwave power-dependent FL spectra clearly indicates multi-colour fluorescence emission, which appears to be produced by a rise in the degree of carbonization and shape change of CQDs as process temperature increases upon increase in microwave power. In general, FL emission studies reveal that CQDs synthesized at 360 W microwave power provide the most intense greenish fluorescence emission. Meanwhile, the primary goal of the current study is to exploit the fluorescence properties of CQDs for potential applications in latent fingerprint and anti-counterfeit detection. Consequently, the microwave process power was optimized to 360 W based on process consistency and repeatability, as well as the aforementioned FL emission properties.

The aqueous solution of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) exhibited remarkable colloidal stability, with no evidence of precipitation over time. This stability was further validated using the Tyndall effect, a well-established method for determining particle dispersibility in colloidal systems. The Tyndall effect relies on the scattering of light by particles in a colloidal suspension, which differentiates a colloidal solution from a true solution, where no light scattering is observed. This phenomenon serves as an indicator of particle presence and stability within the medium. To verify the colloidal nature of the N-P-K@CQDs synthesized under different microwave power conditions, a red laser beam with a wavelength of 650 nm and power output of 5 mW was directed through the solution. In colloidal solutions, the laser beam interacts with individual suspended CQDs, scattering and reflecting the light, which makes the laser beam path visible. In contrast, when the laser was passed through deionized water, no such scattering occurred, confirming the absence of dispersed particles and demonstrating the Tyndall effect, as depicted in Fig. S3 in the supplementary file. The experiment was conducted on N-P-K@CQD samples synthesized at varying microwave power levels, allowing for an assessment of the impact of power on colloidal behavior. After careful evaluation of the light scattering and overall stability, the CQD sample processed at an optimized microwave power of 360 W was determined to offer the best balance between synthesis efficiency and colloidal stability. This sample was subsequently chosen for more in-depth exploration of its morphological, physicochemical, and photophysical properties. The optimized sample provided a foundation for further studies into its potential applications, leveraging its stability and optical properties, such as fluorescence, for use in areas like forensic science, bioimaging, and anti-counterfeiting technologies.

This comprehensive investigation of the colloidal stability of CQDs, confirmed by the Tyndall effect and laser beam scattering, underscores the importance of synthesis conditions on the final properties of CQDs. Moreover, it highlights the potential for CQDs synthesized via an eco-friendly, microwave-assisted approach to achieve stable colloidal dispersions, which is critical for their use in various scientific and industrial applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and physicochemical characterizations of N-P-K@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological characteristics of the optimized N-P-K@CQDs samples (360 W microwave processed) were examined using HRTEM. HRTEM images and related selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns are displayed in Fig. 4 (a)–(f).

The TEM micrographs (Fig. 4(a)-(c)) show that the as-synthesized CQDs are tiny, but due to the absence of a passivation layer, they are poorly dispersed and agglomerated, with a quasi-circular or polygonal form. Meanwhile, the statistical distribution of particles was established, and the resulting histogram is shown in Fig. 4(d). CQDs have an average diameter of 4.7 nm with diameters ranging from 3 to 6.5 nm, demonstrating a considerable quantum confinement effect. Consequently, the thickness (number of graphite layers) distribution and the diameter distribution are stacked in the TEM size distributions. This raises the variability because the orientation of the CQDs affects their distribution on the TEM grid. Moreover, the HRTEM micrographs also demonstrates the presence of particles with distinct lattice fringes, distinguished by a d-spacing, and validates the structural integrity of the N-P-K@CQDs. In fact, the HRTEM images reveal the crystalline nature of the CQDs (Fig. 4(c)), and the fast Fourier transform was used to calculate the lattice spacing (FFT). The observed lattice spacing of 0.33 nm is due to the (002) lattice plane of graphite [38]. The observed d-spacing of N-P-K@CQDs being higher than that of bulk graphite further suggests the turbostratic carbon structure (sp2 deformation) [39,40]. Furthermore, the large diffused ring in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern in Fig. 4(e) demonstrated the poor poly-crystalline structure of the synthesized CQDs.

Energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was used to analyse the elemental composition of the CQDs as they were being created, as shown in Fig. 4(f). In energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy observations, the presence of high carbon (at. percent 70.11) and oxygen (at. percent 23.24) concentrations, as well as trace amounts of nitrogen (at. percent 5.97), phosphorous (at. percent 0.67), and potassium concentrations (at. percent 0.2), suggests that the as-synthesized CQDs are effectively doped with nitrogen, potassium and phosphorus. However, the higher oxygen concentration reveals the possibility of presence of various functionalization.

Hereafter, the elemental composition and surface states of as-synthesized N-P-K@CQDs were also verified using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 5 (a)) shows that C, O, N, K, and P are present, with binding energies of 284.6 eV, 531.5 eV, 399.5 eV, 375.4 eV, and 311.35 eV, respectively. Then, we deconvoluted high-resolution C 1 s spectra to represent sp2C-sp2C, N-sp2C, and C-OH bonds, respectively, using three components with corresponding centres at 284.4 eV, 285.8 eV, and 287.2 eV (see Fig. 5(b) for details). The C = O and C-OH groups are confirmed to exist by similar discoveries made for the O 1 s spectra in Fig. 5(c), which are found to consist of two components with centres at 530.7 eV and 532.2 eV, respectively. Meanwhile, two peaks with centres at 399.3 eV and 401.9 eV can be seen in high-resolution N 1 s spectra (Fig. 5(d)), suggesting the existence of C-N and N-O bonds and demonstrating that nitrogen atoms were efficiently doped in CQDs [41]. The Phosphorus, P 2p peaks exhibited in Fig. 5(e) may be further divided into two peaks, P 2p3/2 and P 2p1/2 , which are centered at 132.5 eV and 135.2 eV, respectively [42]. This shows that P doping in CQDs was successful. The K 2p peaks in Fig. 5(f), which were separated into two peaks with centres at 292.3 eV and 295.1 eV, respectively, and revealed the characteristic K 2p 3/2 and K 2p 1/2 peaks, demonstrate that K doping in CQDs was also successful [43]. Thus, the XPS analysis demonstrates that the environmentally friendly and cost-effective synthesis of CQDs from Pyrostegia venusta flower extract was achieved with effective N, P, and K element doping, producing N,P,K-co-doped CQDs without the addition of other sources for doping. The calculated atomic percentages of C, O, N, K, and P are 70.11 %, 5.97 %, 23.24 %, 0.2 %, and 0.67 %, respectively. These values are almost identical to those found through EDX analysis.

Furthermore, the degree of structural disorder in the carbon matrix, namely the mixing of sp2 and sp3-hybridized carbon atoms, was subjectively and quantitatively assessed using nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy. The sp3 and sp2 hybridized carbon atoms were identified using 13C NMR. The 13C NMR spectra in Fig. S4 (a) in supplementary file show a combination of sp3 and sp2-hybridized carbon atoms, with sp3 atoms outnumbering sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. Two distinct chemical shift (δ) zones were observed; the first peaked at approximately 115 ppm and ranged from 180-200 ppm, with peaks centered at 184.15, 190. The second peaking in the range of 60–80 ppm, relates to sp3-carbons and carbons associated with hydroxyl and amine, and ranged from 67.04, 68.53, 70.49, and 71.15 ppm [44]. Nevertheless, the NMR spectra of 1H shown in Fig. S4 (b) show three different chemical shift (δ) regions: 1–3 ppm (dominated by sp3 C-H protons), 3–6 ppm (linked to hydroxyl group protons), and 8–8.5 ppm (linked to aromatic or sp2 protons).

XRD was used to confirm the crystallinity of the N,P,K co-doped CQDs as they were created, as shown in Fig. S5 in the supplementary file. The observed wide diffraction peak with a centre angle of 23.8°, which corresponds to the (002) plane, was attributed to a turbostatic structure produced by the interlayer packing of disordered carbon atoms in the hexagonal graphite structure of CQDs. The amorphous form of the generated particles had an impact on the broad peak as well. At 29.5°, a larger diffraction peak can be seen, and at 42.2°, 52.3°, and 61.2°, a few smaller peaks can be seen. The extent of graphitization along the (110), (200), and (211) planes is correlated with these peaks (JCPDS Card No. 72–2091) [45].

According to FTIR analyses, there are numerous functional groups and connections present in carbon quantum dots, as depicted in Fig. S6 in the supplementary file. Convincing evidence for the existence of –OH, C = O, and −N = C = O was provided by stretching frequencies of 3421.72, 1656.25, and 1099.43 cm−1, respectively [46]. The artificial carbon quantum dots are very water soluble due to these hydrophilic functional groups. In order to determine the surface charge of the N,P,K-co-doped CQDs as-synthesised, the zeta potential, also known as the electro kinetic potential, was measured using a zetasizer [47]. This method also reveals double layer properties and other properties of CQD with different hydrophilic or hydrophobic functional groups. The size of the zeta potential depends on how stable CQDs particles are over short or long time periods. As seen in Fig. S7 in the supplementary file, a zeta potential of −14.8 mV was found, demonstrating how stable CQDs are in aqueous solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Excitation-wavelength dependent fluorescence characteristics of N-P-K@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: On the other hand, the excitation dependent FL spectra may be useful in creating and putting into practise the down-conversion strategy for optical displays. Excitation-dependent FL emission was therefore studied. However, the highest FL emission was found around 424.6 nm for 310 nm excitation wavelength. As the excitation wavelength was increased from 250 to 535 nm, the emission wavelength shifted towards a higher wavelength from 426.7 to 588.1 nm (see Fig. 6 (a) for detail). On Fig. 6(b), color-coded maps of photoemission are displayed, with the excitation wavelength on the y-axis and the FL intensity on the colour scale. Fluorescence emission peaks shift toward redshift as the excitation wavelength increases, indicating photon re-absorption. The findings demonstrate the presence of excitation-dependent fluorescence in CQDs, which is related to edge defects and the quantum confinement effect.

As shown in Fig. 7 , the CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage) chromaticity coordinates from the CIE 1931 and 1976 conventions were used to determine the colour emitted from fluorescence spectra [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed fluorescence mechanism of Pyrostegia venusta derived N-P-K@ CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Drawing from the aforementioned explanation, a schematic illustrated in Fig. 8 is suggested for the structural transformation of N-P-K@ CQDs in the presence of protic solvent (such as DI water) by an intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) process connected to hydrogen bonds [49,50].

Carbon quantum dots exhibit photoluminescence, which varies with the excitation wavelength. Fluorescence colour is often associated with electronic bandgap fluctuations generated by conjugated π-domains, surface defect states, local fluorophores, and heteroatom doping (in this example, nitrogen, potassium and phosphorus). This work proposes an energy band structure scheme, as seen in Fig. 8(b), based on the XPS, FTIR, and fluorescence spectra of N-P-K@ CQDs produced from Pyrostegia venusta. A variety of chromophoric band-gap structures formed by groups associated with C, N, K and P, such as C-C/C = C (intrinsic-C), C-N (graphitic-N), C-Ca, C = O groups, grain boundaries etc., are clearly visible in the observed excitation dependent FL emission spectra, especially on the lower energy side, as illustrated in Fig. 6 [51]. Conversely, surface defects can act as a site of capture for excitons, leading to surface defect state fluorescence. Surface defects are mostly caused by surface oxidation or dangling bonds. Besides, some of the external factors that influence fluorescence emission are the presence of other dissolved inorganic and organic compounds, temperature, pH, the local concentration of fluorescent species, and interactions between the solvent molecules surrounding the fluorophore (which determine the solvent's polarity). The consequences of these factors might vary greatly depending on the specific fluorophore, however, environmental circumstances can significantly affect the absorption and emission spectra in addition to the quantum yield. The exceptional fluorescence sensitivity really results mostly from the interactions that take place in the surrounding environment when the excited state is in existence. The fluorescence mechanism of CQDs is often attributed to doping components, surface/edge states related with surface functional groups, and quantum confinement. The localized π-electron undergoes absorption of UV photons at a wavelength of 250 nm, which causes it to change from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), producing an exciton. This exciton can emit ultraviolet light upon the completion of vibrational relaxation by radiative energy recombination. In contrast, the excited electron may choose to follow the non-radiative pathway 1 and go through an inter-band transition, which would include a change in LUMO from a higher to a lower one. After this transition, it can emit visible light via routes 2, 3, 4, or 5, depending on the degree of doping (Fig. 8(b) provides a more thorough description). On the other hand, once the fluorophore is excited to the higher vibrational level that corresponds to the first excited singlet state, the extra vibrational energy quickly dissipates to the solvent molecules nearby. As shown in Fig. 8(b), this happens when the fluorophore slowly relaxes to the lowest vibrational energy level, mostly because of strong H-mediated ICT via routes 6, 7, or 8. Solvent molecules reorient around the excited fluorophore more slowly, a process known as solvent relaxation in Fig. 8(b), which helps stabilize and further reduce the excited state energy level. This process reduces the energy difference between excited and ground states, resulting in a redshift, or longer wavelengths at which fluorescence emission occurs. The combination of polarity, locally excited (LE) or ICT processes, and hydrogen bonding stabilized ICT is anticipated to cause the fluorescence emission of the N-P-K@CQDs, subject to the solvent's polarity and the solvent molecules' stability of the excited state. The formation of hydrogen bonds between N-P-K@CQDs and DI water is believed to improve the stability of the excited ICT state (H-bond ICT), hence increasing the red shift of fluorescence emission [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-P-K@CQDs/corn-starch nanocomposite phosphors for latent fingerprint detection
ce:section.ce:para: The principle behind fingerprint recognition relies on the unique ridge patterns found on an individual’s fingertips, which function much like a personal barcode. These patterns, including arches, loops, and whorls, are distinct for each person, even identical twins. When someone touches a surface, they leave behind a latent fingerprint—a trace of sweat, oils, and other substances naturally secreted by the skin. Fingerprint recognition technology captures these latent prints and compares them to a database to verify a person’s identity. The use of carbon quantum dots enhances this process, especially for detecting latent fingerprints that are otherwise invisible. CQDs are nanoscale particles with large surface areas and unique optical properties, such as fluorescence. When applied to a surface suspected of having a fingerprint, CQDs interact with the fingerprint residue through chemical mechanisms like hydrogen bonding or electrostatic forces, allowing them to bind with components such as proteins, lipids, or salts in the fingerprint. This interaction changes the CQDs’ optical properties, typically causing them to fluoresce under ultraviolet (UV) or visible light, making the previously invisible fingerprint visible for capture by a camera or sensor. Once the image is acquired, software analyzes the ridge patterns and other fingerprint features, which are then compared against a stored database for identity matching. CQD-based fingerprint recognition enhances sensitivity, enabling detection of even faint fingerprints, while the selective interaction with residue ensures high specificity and reduces interference. This technique works effectively across various surfaces, from glass to metal and paper, and offers durable visualization of fingerprints, making them suitable for long-term storage and analysis. The CQD-enhanced method significantly improves the precision, sensitivity, and versatility of traditional fingerprint recognition techniques, making it a valuable tool for biometric authentication and forensic applications. In this study, N-P-K@CQDs/corn-starch nanocomposite luminescent powder was used for latent fingerprint development, emitting a greenish color upon binding to fingerprint ridges, activated by exposure to UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm (refer to Fig. S8 in the supplementary file). This change enables the visualization of the previously invisible fingerprint, which is then captured and analyzed using imaging devices. The captured image is compared against a database of known fingerprints to verify identity. CQDs offer several advantages over traditional fingerprint detection methods, including higher sensitivity to faint prints, specificity through selective interaction with fingerprint components, versatility across different surface types like glass, paper, and metal, and durability, allowing for long-term storage of visualized prints. By harnessing the unique properties of CQDs, fingerprint recognition can be made more efficient, improving both security and forensic capabilities.

This activation makes the fingerprint more visible, leading to a clean and vivid picture that allows for quick examination of the print. This approach is useful in forensic science for identifying and examining fingerprints due to its ability to retain latent fingerprints on many surfaces, including porous and non-porous ones. Besides, a portable, transportable, smartphone-based dactyloscope is required to detect LFP at the crime scene and expedite the criminal investigation process. Hence, this study also develops a portable and transportable dactyloscope prototype using a smart phone. Figure S9 depicts the prototype of a custom-made instrument. One important benefit of using this dactyloscope is the ability to develop LFPs using N-P-K@CQDs/corn starch-based luminescent powder first, and then perform fluorescent scanning on a smartphone while the data is electronically saved in the appropriate format and linked with a cyber-police station or central forensic server. A custom-built prototype of a smartphone-based dactyloscope is connected to one of these suggested networks in Figure S10 to identify latent fingerprints at the point of investigation. Latent fingerprints were developed on various solid surfaces such as a laptop, CD, fridge handle, mobile, etc. by lightly brushing the affected area with CQDs@cornstarch composite based fluorescent powder, which was then lifted with scotch tape and pasted on the glass slide (see Fig. 9 ). At the point-of-investigation, the emitted florescence colour from LFPs under UV light illumination (365 nm, 18 W) was determined by taking a digital image with the aid of a smartphone camera.

Recently, Python-based digital processing has grown in importance as an artificial intelligence approach for evaluating LFP images [53]. This method leverages minutiae features—unique points within fingerprint patterns—extracted through sophisticated algorithms. For effective analysis, the original LFP image must exhibit sufficient contrast to ensure that both Level 1 (overall pattern) and Level 2 (minutiae details) features are clearly visible. The image undergoes a multi-stage transformation into a binary model before feature extraction.

First, the input color image is converted to grayscale using a combination of Gaussian blurring and sharpness adjustments (refer to Fig. 10 (a)–(d)). To further refine the image, it is subjected to CLAHE (Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization), which enhances contrast and visibility by controlling contrast amplification while reducing noise (see Fig. 10(e)). This step highlights the key contours necessary for analysis. Next, the image is binarized by classifying each pixel as either “1″ (white) or ”0″ (black) based on grayscale values. This process (shown in Fig. 10(f)) eliminates noise, sharpens blurred areas, and removes shadows, yielding a cleaner, crisper image. Fig. 10(g) then illustrates the Galton-Henry classification of Level 2 LFP features, including bifurcations, spurs, islands, eyes, crossovers, deltas, short ridges, and dots, which have been effectively enhanced and retained [54]. Notably, the fluorescent properties of the LFPs on various surfaces retain their original color, demonstrating that the CQDs' luminescent characteristics are not compromised when in contact with surfaces of varying chemical compositions. The improved LFPs display ridge details clearly, with minimal background interference, highlighting the adaptability and efficacy of this visualization technique for enhancing LFPs on both porous and non-porous surfaces.

In conclusion, integrating N-P-K@CQDs/cornstarch nanocomposite phosphors with Python-based AI processing significantly enhances LFP detection, offering superior accuracy and clarity compared to traditional methods. This hybrid approach not only refines the visibility of key fingerprint features but also ensures consistent performance across different surface types.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-P-K@CQDs as anti-counterfeiting ink
ce:section.ce:para: It is a widespread problem when high-value items like jewellery, clothing, electronics, pharmaceuticals, legal documents, banknotes, and other legal documents are counterfeited. Numerous anti-counterfeiting tactics have been developed that make use of a range of components and verification techniques to combat counterfeits [55]. In a variety of security applications, anti-counterfeiting tags based on luminescent nanoparticles hold great promise, and numerous varieties of such security inks have recently been found.

Thus, in this work, luminous security inks based on N-P-K@CQDs were also examined. The unique optical quality of security ink may be used to verify the object's legitimacy. On non-luminescent commercial drawing paper, symbols such as “O,” “A,” “M,” and “N” were painted with as-synthesised CQDs to showcase the efficacy of CD fluorescence-lifetime-encoded inks as anti-counterfeiting tags. Different painted symbols' pixels produce a similar green colour emission that can only be seen when excited with a 365 nm UV-A tube (see Fig. 11 for detail). The security colour paint from the well-known cartoon character Doraemon displays a greenish fluorescence under UVA light that is otherwise not visible in broad daylight. Finally, tables S2 and S3 provide an overview of the different technologies and methods used, along with the key characteristics of CQDs utilized in LFPs and anti-counterfeiting applications. These tables offer a concise summary of the progress made in CQD-based phosphors across these fields. Since CQDs have special properties that make them very promising for use in multidimensional applications, we believe that N-P-K@CQDs derived from Pyrostegia venusta and its nanocomposite phosphors will be very promising nanomaterials in the near future for forensic applications, particularly in anti-counterfeiting or rapid latent fingerprints detection.

The limitations of this study and potential future directions warrant careful consideration. One notable limitation is the restricted range of surface types tested for the CQD-based fingerprint-developing powder. Although the powder demonstrated efficacy on several surfaces, its performance on a broader array of forensic substrates remains unexamined. Additionally, the study did not extensively assess the environmental stability of the powder, including its resistance to variations in humidity, temperature, and chemical exposure [56–59]. There is also scope for improving the fluorescence properties and quantum yield of the CQDs to enhance their sensitivity and visibility. While the study effectively demonstrated the feasibility of the CQD-based powder on a small scale, further research is needed to scale up the production process and evaluate its cost-effectiveness for commercial applications. Besides, one of the key challenges in CQD research is the lack of standardization in synthesis, characterization, and application, making it difficult to ensure consistency and reproducibility across studies. While small-scale CQD synthesis is relatively simple, scaling up for commercial use remains a major hurdle. Further dedicated research is needed to develop standardized protocols and cost-efficient, scalable methods that ensure the quality and functionality of CQDs for large-scale industrial applications [14,16,60]. Moreover, the integration of the smartphone-based dactyloscope prototype with existing forensic systems and workflows was not explored. Future research should address these limitations by testing the powder on a wider range of surfaces, evaluating its durability under diverse environmental conditions, optimizing CQD properties, and investigating large-scale production and economic viability. Additionally, exploring how the dactyloscope can be integrated into established forensic technologies and databases could further enhance its practical utility. Addressing these aspects will contribute to advancing the field of forensic science and security technologies, improving the effectiveness and applicability of CQD-based methods.

In conclusion, this study successfully demonstrated an eco-friendly, cost-effective, and simple microwave-assisted synthesis of luminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) doped with hetero-atoms (N-P-K@CQDs), utilizing Pyrostegia venusta (begonia) flower extract. The process eliminates the need for external doping chemicals, leveraging the intrinsic phytochemicals, vitamins, and minerals present in the flower extract to produce CQDs with excellent optical and physicochemical properties. The synthesized N-P-K@CQDs were characterized through a comprehensive suite of techniques including HRTEM, EDX, SAED, XPS, XRD, NMR, FTIR, Zeta potential, UV–vis, and photoluminescence spectroscopy, which revealed the nanoparticles' spherical morphology with an average diameter of 4.7 nm, a quantum yield of approximately 25 %, and exceptional water solubility and photostability. The fluorescence characterization exhibited excitation-wavelength dependence, with redshifts observed in fluorescence emission peaks as the excitation wavelength increased, indicating photon re-absorption. This phenomenon is attributed to quantum confinement effects and edge defects, contributing to the observed excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior. The color emitted by the CQDs' fluorescence was analyzed and defined using CIE chromaticity coordinates (1931 and 1976), providing insights into the fluorescence mechanism. Furthermore, the synthesized N-P-K@CQDs were incorporated into cornstarch to develop a nanocomposite phosphor powder (N-P-K@CQDs/cornstarch) that emitted greenish fluorescence when exposed to UV light at 365 nm. This nanocomposite was successfully applied as a fluorescent marker in the detection of latent fingerprints (LFPs), utilizing a custom-built smartphone-based dactyloscope, which offered a portable, real-time point-of-care solution for forensic investigations. The N-P-K@CQDs/cornstarch nanocomposite phosphor powder demonstrated high sensitivity, contrast, and resolution in fingerprint visualization across various porous and non-porous substrates of forensic importance. Additionally, a Python-based digital image processing algorithm was employed to enhance and retain critical minutiae in LFPs, further improving the efficacy of fingerprint analysis. The findings also extended beyond forensic applications to the development of anti-counterfeiting technologies. The study presented a proof-of-concept for anti-counterfeiting tags utilizing N-P-K@CQDs-based inks, offering an innovative approach to enhance product security. The exceptional optical properties, biocompatibility, and color tunability of N-P-K@CQDs make them highly suitable for such applications, providing a scalable, non-toxic alternative to traditional fluorescent markers. The broader impact of these findings is significant across several fields, particularly in nanotechnology, forensic science, and anti-counterfeiting. The sustainable synthesis approach not only addresses environmental concerns associated with traditional CQD production methods but also demonstrates the versatility and practical utility of CQDs in real-world applications. In forensics, the integration of CQDs with cornstarch for LFP detection represents a major advancement, offering a portable and efficient method for on-site crime scene investigations. Meanwhile, the application of CQDs in anti-counterfeiting technology opens up new avenues for enhancing security measures in commercial industries, addressing the growing issue of counterfeit goods. Overall, this study contributes to the development of sustainable, scalable, and cost-effective CQD-based technologies, with potential for widespread commercial and forensic applications. The insights gained from the comprehensive characterization and application of N-P-K@CQDs pave the way for further innovations in CQD synthesis and their utilization in emerging sectors, driving future research toward more advanced, environmentally friendly nanomaterials.

Gajal Singla: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Vishal Kansay: Methodology, Investigation. Varun Dutt Sharma: Methodology, Investigation.
